headlines.title: Man arrested after hidden camera found inside restroom at San Jose coffee shop
headlines.description: A 38-year-old San Jose man was arrested earlier this month on suspicion of placing a hidden camera inside a public restroom at a downtown coffee shop, police said Thursday. David Perales allegedly installed the hidden camera in the bathroom at a coffee shop along the 100 block of East Santa Clara Street on June 9, according to police. A coffee shop employee found the camera on the same day and reported the discovery to police. Detectives, with the help of the police department’s Real Time Intelligence Center and automated license plate readers, identified Perales as the primary suspect and obtained an arrest warrant, police said. Perales was arrested in San Jose on July 2 and booked into Santa Clara County Main Jail for invasion of privacy, according to police.

headlines.title: Death of woman in Brentwood police custody was accidental, coroner's inquest rules
headlines.description: The 2025 death of a woman following her arrest by Brentwood police officers has been ruled accidental by a coroner’s inquest, the Contra Costa County Sheriff’s Office said Wednesday. An inquest is a process separate from a medical examiner’s probe into an in-custody death and is when a jury reviews witness testimony and determines the manner of the cause of an individual’s death. “The finding of the jury is that the manner of death is accident,” said the sheriff’s office on Wednesday. On Sept. 26, 2025, 72-year-old Yolanda Ramirez was arrested by Brentwood Police Officers Aaron Peachman and Danielle Tjhia following a dispute with her sister. One week later, she died. According to the footage, Ramirez, who suffered from diabetes, was allegedly forcibly pushed into the back of a police vehicle by officers after hitting her head on the car, according to a federal civil rights lawsuit filed by Ramirez’s family in January. After being left alone for 20 minutes, body camera footage allegedly shows Ramirez was found barely conscious and slumped over when officers returned to check on her. She died following emergency surgery for brain bleed on Oct. 3. Brentwood Feb 27 Brentwood Jan 1 According to the sheriff’s office, jurors were advised to choose between four possible rulings: Accident, suicide, natural causes and “at the hands of another person, other than by accident.” Jurors found that Ramirez’s death happened by accident and was a possible byproduct of someone’s action. Melissa Nold, attorney for Ramirez’s family, said that she’s pleased with the outcome and hopes to see the district attorney pursue charges against Peachman and Tjhia. “With natural causes, it’s almost impossible to file a charge,” she said. “The accidental finding still gives us something for the district attorney to use. You can be held liable.” A coroner’s report released in 2025 concluded that Ramirez died of natural causes, not an accident, according to Nold. Following the coroner’s autopsy, the family ordered a second, independent autopsy funded by the Colin Kaepernick Foundation, which pays for medical examinations of people who have died after police encounters. That autopsy determined Ramirez died of injuries during the arrest, said Nold.

headlines.title: San Francisco overdose deaths down over this time last year, city says
headlines.description: San Francisco experienced fewer overdose deaths in the first half of this year compared to the first half of 2025, the Department of Public Health said Wednesday. Dr. Jeffrey Hom and Dr. Naveena Bobba from the Department of Public Health spoke at a press conference on Wednesday to discuss the statistics around the declining rates. From January to June of this year, there have been 262 overdose deaths, a 27% decline compared to the same time frame in 2025, according to the city. “This downward trend is encouraging but the number of people that continue to die from overdoses is still unacceptable,” said Bobba. “It’s not enough to prevent deaths, we want people to flourish.” The decline is attributed partly to an increase in treatment capacity. San Francisco has increased treatment-on-demand and other supports. The city also received $100 million in funding from 2024’s Proposition 1, a statewide behavioral health measure that provides money for things like substance abuse disorder treatment. These funds went towards more beds added to the USCF Health Hyde Hospital and the Treasure Island Behavioral Health Center. In 2025, the city also opened a 24/7 stabilization center where doctors, nurses, and behavioral health specialists can treat people experiencing a crisis resulting from substance use or drug reactions. “We have really done a lot of work to try to get our treatment capacity increased, really reaching out to ensure that people have access to the treatment that best suits them,” said Bobba. Officials also noted that San Francisco reflects national trends which also point towards an overall decline in accidental overdose deaths.
headlines.source: Lloyd B. Davis | Bay City News

headlines.title: 11,000 California drivers will have to retake written test. Here's why
headlines.description: Thousands of licensed drivers in California are being required to retake their knowledge tests because the California Department of Motor Vehicles says it found “irregularities” in their test results and suspects them of cheating, officials said today. Last month, the DMV sent letters to some 11,000 drivers saying that their written test results showed “irregularities.” The department did not provide specifics about what problems were found with the tests. Those receiving the letters were instructed to return to a DMV office and retake their knowledge test within 30 days. Individuals who do not complete and pass the retest will have their driver’s license cancelled, the DMV said. California Mar 10 The DMV indicated that some individuals may have attempted to cheat during the testing process. “The California DMV has identified irregularities in certain driver knowledge test results that may indicate instances of cheating,” a department spokesperson said. Through “routine internal monitoring,” the department identified patterns in driver knowledge test results indicating that “some individuals may have attempted to circumvent the testing process using various cheating methods,” the representative said. The DMV emphasized that although certain test results have been identified as “potentially problematic,” receipt of a notice alone does not indicate a final determination that cheating occurred in that instance. “Nonetheless, these irregularities are test taker related and not the result of an internal DMV technical issue, or involvement of artificial intelligence,” the spokesperson said. “Ensuring the integrity of the knowledge testing process is essential to public safety and to confirm that drivers understand California’s rules of the road.” Several cases have been referred to county district attorneys for prosecution, according to the DMV. “DMV is not sharing additional information at this time, so as not to reveal investigative methods and protect the integrity of the investigative process,” the department said Tuesday.

headlines.title: Man who died after boat capsized in San Francisco Bay identified
headlines.description: A passenger who died after being on a vessel that capsized and sank Tuesday in the San Francisco Bay was identified Wednesday by the San Francisco Medical Examiner’s Office. Clifford Boisa, 79, of Sutter County, was identified as the victim of the sinking near Alcatraz Island. Family said Boise would’ve been 80 on Sept. 5. According to Calpers, Boisa was a budget analyst for the Department of Fish and Wildlife and retired in 2001. Clifford Boisa was the older brother of the man who owned the boat, John Boisa. John sent the following statement to NBC Bay Area: “I loved Clifford with all of my heart. In addition to being a brother, he was a mentor and role model to me growing up. I cherished him, and much of the person I am was formed by his example. The loss of him is a crushing anguish.” The Boisa family provided the following statement Wednesday to NBC Bay Area: “We and many families have suffered a horrific loss. We deeply appreciate the outpouring of condolences and prayers. At this incredibly difficult time, we ask that you respect our privacy so that we can process and grieve.” On Tuesday at 3:37 p.m., multiple 911 callers reported a vessel in distress in the Bay. The U.S. Coast Guard, as well as police and fire departments from San Francisco, Oakland, Richmond and Tiburon, responded with ground and water assets. At the scene, the vessel was identified as a 50-foot cabin cruiser out of Stockton, according to the San Francisco Fire Department. A total of 20 people were aboard, with 17 being rescued and three individuals that remain missing. Boisa was one of the 17 found during the water rescue. A boat tracking site and a post on social media showed the boat was in McCovey Cove for at least part of the Giants game on Sunday. According to the San Francisco Fire Department, Boisa was rescued from the water but required immediate life-saving measures. He ultimately succumbed to his injuries and died Tuesday. The fire department said a dog also died aboard the vessel, and public safety agencies are continuing to search for the three missing individuals. San Francisco Jul 15 San Francisco Jul 14 1 dead, 3 missing after 50-foot cabin cruiser capsizes near Alcatraz
headlines.source: Bay City News, Marianne Favro and Bigad Shaban

headlines.title: Coast Guard suspends search for 3 people after boat sinks in San Francisco Bay
headlines.description: U.S. Coast Guard crews have suspended a search late Wednesday for three people missing after a boat carrying 20 family members and friends as part of a memorial service capsized a day earlier in the cold, choppy waters of San Francisco Bay near Alcatraz Island, authorities said. U.S. Coast Guard Captain Jared S. Toczko said rescuers have cumulatively scoured 950 square nautical miles (3,260 square kilometers) and the search continued until sundown on Wednesday. Toczko would not dismiss the possibility that those missing could still be alive, though he also said some could have been trapped inside the three-deck, 49-foot (15-meter) cabin cruiser. “We do know individuals were in the main deck and potentially below deck,” he said. But now that the search has been suspended, the U.S. Coast Guard says recovering the boat is the next step in the investigation. Because of the depth of the water, that process could take time and may require an underwater drone before investigators can determine exactly what caused the boat to capsize. After the search was suspended, San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie released the following statement: “The U.S. Coast Guard has suspended its search and rescue mission for the three missing people following yesterday’s boat accident in the San Francisco Bay. My heart goes out to their loved ones. This accident is heartbreaking, and we grieve alongside those affected. I want to recognize SFFD, SFPD, the U.S. Coast Guard, and our partners from Oakland, Marin, and across the region. Our first responders have conducted an intensive operation over more than 24 hours using all manner of resources and technology, even with support from private citizens, to try to find all individuals involved. Because of their dedicated work, 16 people were saved after the boat capsized. I want to again extend my gratitude to the San Francisco Human Services Agency and the American Red Cross for supporting the survivors.” One person was pulled from the water but later died, and 16 others were rescued Tuesday afternoon after the boat took on water, listed heavily to one side and then rolled over before sinking, Toczko said. The passengers on board were all close friends and family members, he said. A dog also died. Crews have identified the location the boat sank but have yet to determine how deep it sank, Toczko said. Once the boat is located, officials will send either divers or an underwater drone to determine if it’s feasible to salvage it, said San Francisco Police Commander Brien Hoo. If the boat is under 120 feet (37 meters) of water, it would be difficult for divers to get to it, he said. Witnesses reported “rough seas,” San Francisco Fire Department Chief Dean Crispen said, and rescuers said swells reached up to 5 feet (1.5 meters). Marine weather conditions, however, didn’t warrant a small craft advisory from the National Weather Service. Sudden immersion in water under 60 degrees Fahrenheit (15 degrees Celsius) can lead to cold water shock, a condition where people lose dexterity in minutes. That can be dangerous or deadly when trying to escape a sinking watercraft. Man who died after boat capsized in San Francisco Bay identified The person who died was identified as Clifford Joseph Boisa, 79, from rural Sutter County in the Sacramento Valley, the San Francisco medical examiner said. The Boisa family provided the following statement Wednesday to NBC Bay Area: “We and many families have suffered a horrific loss. We deeply appreciate the outpouring of condolences and prayers. At this incredibly difficult time, we ask that you respect our privacy so that we can process and grieve.” Clifford Boisa was a budget analyst for the Department of Fish and Wildlife, and retired in 2001, according to Calpers. He also served as a Sutter County reserve deputy sheriff from 1987 until 2011. Clifford is the older brother of the man who owned the boat, John Boisa, who provided the following statement to NBC Bay Area: “I loved Clifford with all of my heart. In addition to being a brother, he was a mentor and role model to me growing up. I cherished him, and much of the person I am was formed by his example. The loss of him is a crushing anguish.” What we know about the boat that capsized in the San Francisco Bay Fire department spokesperson Lt. Mariano Elias said the 50-foot vessel, named Volare, was registered out of Stockton, which sits at the eastern edge of the Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta. Tockzo said there were life jackets onboard the boat and that some people were rescued wearing them, though witnesses said several people were in the water without them. “The wind was coming underneath the Golden Gate and blowing toward Alcatraz,” Lt. Joseph England of the Richmond Police Department, who responded to the scene, said Wednesday. “If you have a smaller vessel and you don’t know what you’re doing and you’re hitting those swells sideways, it can lead to disaster.” England said no one was aboard the boat by the time his department’s marine unit got there. The crew found the vessel nearly submerged, with cushions, chairs and other debris floating nearby. Like a ‘Titanic’ scene, rescuer says Two rescuers who jumped into action while fishing for halibut said the boat that sank was more than capable of being out in the bay. Justin Marceline and Michael Montoya said they saw what they thought was smoke and arrived to find the vessel halfway submerged. “We just started yanking people out,” Marceline said. At least two people bobbed in the water without life jackets, while others clung to a windsurfer’s board. Marceline could see people trapped inside the rapidly sinking boat through its windows. He threw lead fishing weights to survivors in the water, hoping they could smash the glass, but they were too weak. “It was like Titanic in real life,” he said. “There was stuff everywhere. People were banging on the glass.” Montoya estimated they pulled eight or nine people aboard, including the captain, before first responders arrived. High-tech tools used in search The U.S. Coast Guard was leading the search effort with a cutter named the Barracuda, other vessels and a fixed-wing aircraft, Petty Officer Kenneth Wiese said Wednesday. The Coast Guard said it would suspend active search operations at sunset Wednesday. “We want to consider every single option,” Wiese said. Search teams were using thermal imaging, tide prediction and modeling to guide their efforts, the fire department said Wednesday. By Tuesday evening, authorities had been searching the open ocean west of the Golden Gate Bridge. Where was the boat coming from? A marine tracking site shows the Volare left the Village West Marina in Stockton on Saturday on its way to the Bay. It was not known how many people were onboard during that portion of the trip. The boat was also in McCovey Cove for at least part of the San Francisco Giants game on Sunday, according to a boat tracking website and a post on social media. The vessel also showed up in an image of the stadium during the broadcast. The boat days later departed near the St. Francis Yacht Club in San Francisco, passed under the Golden Gate Bridge twice and visited Angel Island State Park in the bay before the apparent return trip, according to the ship-tracking website VesselFinder. Angel Island, the largest natural island in the bay, is known for sweeping views of the city. “Our hearts go out to the friends and family of those on board the boat that sunk off the waters off Alcatraz Island,” the St. Francis Yacht Club said in a statement to NBC Bay Area. “While the boat did not embark from St. Francis Yacht Club, nor were there members on board, we share this Bay and therefore, understand the weight of this tragedy.” San Francisco 24 hours ago San Francisco Jul 14 Dispatch audio of initial response to sinking boat near Alcatraz Island Initial callers reported what appeared to be smoke coming from the boat, but San Francisco police officers who first reached the vessel determined it was steam. San Francisco fire officials confirmed Wednesday that the boat that capsized on the Bay was equipped with life vests – although dispatchers quoted witnesses as indicating some people in the water may have been holding onto them instead of wearing them at the time they were spotted. Elias said dispatch records indicate that about 10 minutes after the initial call, a dispatch note indicates that a witness saw “a cluster” of four people in the water holding onto a sail board. The dispatch notation suggests that they were not using life vests for floatation, according to Elias. “We did hear that people were holding onto vests, instead of wearing them,” Elias said. The boat sank about 128 feet down, because it was in a shipping channel, Elias said. He noted that dive teams are typically only allowed to go down about 60 feet into the water, leaving the wreckage of the boat unsearched at this time. He said salvage crews will ultimately bring up the boat as part of the investigation. The maximum-security federal prison at Alcatraz Island, which closed more than 60 years ago, was infamously inescapable due to the chilly waters and strong currents that surround “The Rock.” Designed to hold the nation’s intractable criminals, including Al Capone and George “Machine Gun” Kelly, it is now a popular tourist attraction. The island is about a mile off San Francisco. Associated Press writers Kathy McCormack in Concord, New Hampshire; Ed White in Detroit; Jaimie Ding in Los Angeles; and photographer Noah Berger in San Francisco contributed to this story. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.
headlines.source: Marianne Favro, Hilda Gutierrez, Sergio Quintana, Bigad Shaban, Jaxon Van Derbeken, Brendan Weber, Kristofer Noceda, Olga R. Rodriguez | The Associated Press and Pete Suratos

headlines.title: Drone's role in Dixie Fire may cost PG&E ratepayers
headlines.description: It may end up being the most expensive drone flight in California history. Back in July 2021, an unidentified drone hovered over the Dixie fire origin, just long enough to ground air crews as they were starting to make headway. Now, PG&E wants regulators to allow it to charge nearly $700 million in claims for a fire its equipment started, saying the drone is responsible for the ensuing fire damage. “It came at the wrong time,” said Butte County District Attorney Mike Ramsey, whose office led a multi-county state probe of the fire that broke out on July 13 but raged for weeks. “It was getting on towards evening and a drone showed up,” Ramsey said in a recent interview. “The policy, appropriately so, of Cal Fire is when there’s a drone or something obstructing their flight path, they have to call the flight off.” With air crews grounded to avoid hitting the drone, nightfall came, and winds picked up, Ramsey remembered. “We had our second largest wildfire in United States history.” What was a two-acre fire eventually mushroomed to nearly one million acres, leveling more than 1,000 buildings. The total cost was put at $1.8 billion. Cal Fire’s final report blamed PG&E for failing to cut down a diseased tree that fell on a power line. Ramsey says Cal Fire officials told him that were initially confident they could put out the fire, before the drone showed up, hovered a few moments, then left, leaving air crews helpless as darkness fell. Five years later, just who was flying the drone remains a mystery. But its role in hindering the fire response is being considered by state regulators in deciding whether PG&E, or ratepayers, will have to pay nearly $700 million dollars in remaining damage claims. PG&E’s insurance previously paid $500 million in damages and the state’s wildfire fund has accounted for $600 million in claims, according to the utility. In a filing with the Public Utilities Commission – the state agency that regulates rates, PG&E notes that a state law allows them to charge ratepayers for fires fueled by “factors beyond their control.” Factors like that drone. In the filing, the utility says the “premature grounding” of the air attack due to the drone, “impeded early fire-suppression efforts, enabling a relatively small fire to spread out of control.” PG&E would not comment on the pending regulatory filing but stressed it was found to be a “prudent operator” in the timespan before the fire, having adopted various fire-risk cutting measures. The utility said it was, under the law, to seek rate hikes to cover costs. “The amount PG&E requests in the application reflects the cost of providing utility services, particularly in areas with heightened wildfire risk,” the utility said in a statement. While he understands the drone likely hindered the firefight, Ramsey rejects the logic behind PG&E’s regulatory filing. “I’m very disappointed in PG&E for doing such an argument,” the veteran prosecutor said. “That’s like saying: ‘Okay, there’s a fire and unfortunately the fire engine gets a flat tire and can’t get there in time and the fire gets away — and so therefore, the person that sets the fire shouldn’t be responsible for the fire.’’’ “That is insane, both morally, logically, and legally,” Ramsey concluded. What makes the argument so troubling, Ramsey says, is that the utility had been making progress over the last five years in showing its commitment to safety and change.

headlines.title: ‘It's what we do': Nearby captains rush to help sinking boat in San Francisco Bay
headlines.description: On Tuesday, when a vessel capsized in the San Francisco Bay and started sinking, nearby captains and crew members rushed towards the emergency, pulling people from the water, throwing life jackets to survivors and helping before first responders could reach everyone. A family memorial became a desperate fight for survival as nearby captains dropped everything and raced toward the sinking boat off Alcatraz Island. “Our guys, being there, being ready, helping out. It’s what we do out there. We take care of the Bay,” said Erik Ostrander. “Everyone went there and started providing help.” Among them, Captain Aaron Anfinson and crewmember Jeff DeJung. They were in the middle of a private cruise with 32 passengers on board when they turned their boat toward the emergency. San Francisco Jul 15 San Francisco 24 hours ago “I had my crewmember Jeff grab the fire hose and told my passengers to get inside,” Aaron Anfinson said. “As we were going there, I called the Coast Guard, told them about it. They had people there pretty quickly after we showed up.” Before rescue crews could reach everyone, Anfinson and his crew started getting life-saving equipment into the water. “I didn’t see a life raft anywhere, didn’t see many people with life jackets, so I told Jeff to get life jackets for people on board,” Anfinson said. One woman was struggling to stay above the water. “I threw the life ring to her. She was able to grab onto it and we pulled her in and right over there, on the side of the boat, we can open up that railing there, and we used the swim ladder and we used all sorts of things, not normally emergency-related, and she was able to climb up the ladder, and we got her onto the boat,” DeJung said. “She was bleeding quite a bit. So, we brought her on board, used the ladder to get her on the side of the boat,” Anfinson said. Despite the quick actions of nearby boaters and first responders, one person died and three others remain missing. It’s something Anfinson says is still difficult to accept. “It’s very sad. We did what we could, but it’s very unfortunate,” Anfinson said. For everyone who answered the call, the rescue may have ended on the water, but what they witnessed followed them home Tuesday night. “I gave my mom a big hug when I got home,” DeJung said. The Coast Guard says crews will continue searching through this evening for the three people still missing, using boats, helicopters and aircraft across San Francisco Bay. They also thanked the nearby captains and crews who rushed in to help—saying their quick actions played an important role in rescuing so many people.

headlines.title: More than 3,000 Bay Area customers are without power in latest PG&E PSPS
headlines.description: Thousands of Pacific Gas and Electric customers in the Bay Area were without power Thursday morning due to Public Safety Power Shutoffs initiated by the utility. PG&E said the PSPS was carried out to prevent wildfire, citing the risk of high winds and dry conditions. Here is the breakdown of customers impacted by the PSPS within the Bay Area, according to PG&E: San Francisco: 1. Peninsula: 1. East Bay: 1505. South Bay: 17. North Bay: 2,052. In a neighborhood off of Kirker Pass Road, just past the Concord Pavilion, neighbors said their power went off at around 3 p.m. in their area. PG&E anticipates power will be restored to regions in or near the Bay Area by Thursday afternoon that were affected by planned outages, the utility said. . Unlike previous outages that have happened in the area, neighbors said they did get text alerts from PG&E in advance that this outage would be coming. Many neighbors suspect large power lines on a nearby grassy hillside could present a fire risk. Several recalled a similar outage that lasted for more than a day a couple of years ago. “It’s a common thing for this particular area. I understand the need for safety, I appreciate that concern,” said Brandon Stracener, whose home was without power Wednesday afternoon and evening. “There is an impression, I think, here that anytime there’s a five-mile-an-hour or higher wind, PG&E panics and shuts the power off. And I have to wonder if there are other things they could be doing to make things safer that require a little bit more investment in energy,” Stracener continued. Stracener was considering a late-night drive back to his office to complete the work he couldn’t do at home without power. PG&E said it works to limit shutoffs to only the areas where conditions make them necessary. “So we want to, again, make sure that we’re only shutting off power where we need to. And each of those locations has a different shutoff time based on the unsafe weather that’s forecasted,” said Dave Canny, PG&E Vice President for the North Coast Region, at a press conference earlier in the day on Wednesday. But for the people who are experiencing the shutoffs, that does change the fact that they had to face a hot summer afternoon and night without power. PG&E does list resource centers that are open for impacted residents to go to for ice and to charge their devices. However, neighbors in Concord felt frustrated that their nearest options were to go to Tracy or to Mill Valley, both more than 40 miles away. PG&E also initiated Public Safety Power Shutoffs back in May of this year, which impacted hundreds of customers in Contra Costa County.

headlines.title: Stolen French Bulldog from San Jose still missing
headlines.description: Police continue to search for a French Bulldog stolen during a home burglary in San Jose. The dog named “Lady” was taken in May along with thousands of dollars in cash, police said. One suspect in the dognapping was arrested last month in Livermore. Lady has grey fur and a pink scar under her chin. SJPD is asking for assistance from the public to locate the dog.

headlines.title: Officials go door-to-door in San Jose to retrieve Costco plants tied to invasive pest
headlines.description: Agricultural officials hit San Jose Wednesday, trying to retrieve about 1,300 grapevine plants purchased at Costco that may be infested by the “glassy-winged sharpshooter” insect. Agricultural workers were once again going door-to-door, this time in San Jose, to the homes of people who bought some of the 1300 grapevine plants from Costco. As in south county, workers are leaving bags with instructions for scheduled pickups. “There’s a much higher density of people here who own the plants. So, we’ve got a lot more people in this area that we have to get to, to send out this information so we can get these plants back,” Associate Biologist Standards Specialist Vivian Volpe said. The plants are being examined in a lab at the Agricultural Division offices. Researchers are looking for the glassy-winged sharpshooter, including the type of eggs observed under a microscope. The dark spots are the eyes of the insect. The main concern is that the pest can carry “pierce’s disease”, the real danger to crops. “It’s not just grapevines alone that they infest. They can also go to, say, a citrus, to local trees like the crape myrtle. There are myriads of different other types of plants and trees that they can infest and harbor and stay there. And then, those plants can become carriers of the disease. So, if left unchecked, they can spread and keep spreading,” Agricultural Biologist Khoi Nguyen Said. Agricultural crews were out in Gilroy again on Wednesday collecting bags people left out after being notified Monday. The deputy agricultural commissioner says they’re making good progress, but there will be more to do even after all the plants have been collected. “The next phase from that would be a ‘monitoring phase’ where we would be placing glassy-winged sharpshooter traps at regular intervals throughout the county to detect any insects that would have come off of the grapevines and gotten out in the community,” SCC Deputy Agricultural Commissioner John Sorfleet said. One person who turned in their plant was relieved. “I was worried about all the plants. It can go around the block,” Gilroy resident Angelica Sanchez said. It will take at least a couple of months to complete retrieving all the grapevines. The Agricultural Division is asking anyone who bought the grapevine plants but has not been contacted to please call their office.

headlines.title: Health officials look into cyclosporiasis outbreak as cases increase nationwide
headlines.description: Health officials are still investigating a multi-state outbreak of cyclosporiasis, a parasitic illness that can cause prolonged, severe diarrhea. The CDC says more than 1,600 cases have been confirmed, with another 5,100 under investigation, for a total of nearly 7,000 possible cases in 34 states. Although California is part of those 34 states, state health officials said they aren’t seeing an increase in cases. On the other hand, Michigan has reported the largest number of illnesses. Maria Aguilo was one of those cases. She says she started feeling sick in late June. After multiple doctor visits and tests, she was finally diagnosed with cyclosporiasis almost three weeks later. She’s now on antibiotics and recovering. “I suddenly started having uncontrollable diarrhea, nausea and vomiting. I also had really intense stomach cramps and pain,” she said. “It took 18 days of me having every single symptom until I was able to get a diagnosis and start treatment.” “I was in the bathroom maybe between six to eight times an hour,” she continued. As investigators search for the source, Michigan health officials say there’s a strong early signal pointing to lettuce. Aguilo says she still doesn’t know exactly where she was exposed. “I have no idea. The only two things I constantly eat are bagged romaine lettuce hearts when I make salads, and I usually have strawberries every breakfast,” she said. UCSF Infectious Disease Specialist Dr. Peter Chin-Hong says washing produce helps, but it isn’t foolproof. “Washing is not enough, but it reduces the risk by at least 50%,” he said. “Fruits like oranges, bananas, melons and mangos, they have skin so you take out the skin and you are gonna be at a very low risk.” The advice from Dr. Chin-Hong: when possible, choose fruits you can peel. If you’re buying lettuce, skip the bagged greens, buy a whole head of lettuce, and remove the outer leaves before eating it.
